What it means: The champion itself performs the action — the default.
The unit this champion summoned acts. Use this when the champion has ONE summon, so there is nothing to disambiguate.
NOT USED DIRECTLY — the pattern. When a champion has MORE THAN ONE summon, name the step that created the one acting, exactly as 'Step N Projectile' does. Store the literal ('Step 1 Summon').
The summon created by step 1 (Yorick's ghoul horde).
The summon created by step 3 (Yorick's BIG ghoul).
The projectile step 1 fired — its impact is what acts (a burst, an on-hit AOE).
The thing step 1 spawned, where that thing is a HITBOX rather than a unit — Vex's shadow arrives, then detonates.
An allied champion performs it (Sejuani rides an ally).
The enemy a previous step knocked back — its flying body is the hitbox (K'Sante).
The bomb a previous step stuck to the victim goes off. It names the EFFECT TYPE (Status / Delayed Blast), not one champion's card — the victim does not explode, the bomb does.

Action Source: Action Source names the thing that ACTS in a step, which is not always the champion. It became a validated vocabulary in round 14 because it had drifted into two kinds of value: structural keys (Self, Summon, Step N Projectile) alongside champion pet names (Dreadway, Child of the Star, Shadow). The pet names were not sloppiness — plain "Summon" cannot say WHICH summon acts when a champion has two, which is what Yorick and Gangplank both hit. The fix keeps that distinction but spells it the way projectiles already did: name the STEP that produced the actor.
